questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:**
The Battle of Plassey, 1757 laid the foundation of
the British empire in India.
**Statement II:**
The Battle of Plassey ousted the French and
established the military supremacy of the English
in Bengal.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct,
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct, but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Battle of Plassey (1957) that is regarded as the beginning of British rule in India**. The Battle of Plassey had political significance for **it laid the foundation of the British empire in India**; it has been rightly regarded as the starting point of British rule in India. **So, Statement I is correct.**
● **The battle established the military supremacy of the English in Bengal as their main rivals, the** **French, were ousted**. They obtained a grant of territories for the maintenance of a properly equipped military force, and their prestige increased manifold. **So, Statement-II is correct.** **So, both Statement-I and Statement-II are correct and Statement-II explains Statement-I** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
With reference to the Treaty of Allahabad (1765),
consider the following statements:
I. 	 Nawab Shuja-ud-Daula surrendered
Allahabad and Kara to the British East India
Company.
II. 	 Mughal emperor Shah Alam II, issued a
farman granting the diwani rights of Bengal,
Bihar and Orissa to the East India Company.
III. 	East India Company received the right to
administer civil justice and discharge police
functions of Bengal.
IV. 	 Nawab Shuja-ud-Daula left his claim over
Awadh, and it was annexed by the British
East India Company.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**: The English forces under Major Hector Munro defeated the combined armies of Mir Kasim, the Nawab of Awadh and Shah Alam II at Buxar on October 22, 1764. The victory made the English a great power in northern India and contenders for the supremacy over the whole country.
● **As per the Treaty of Allahabad,** **Shuja-ud-Daula** was made to surrender Allahabad and Kara **to the** **Mughal Emperor Shah Alam II**. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● It granted the British East India Company the **Diwani rights** over Bengal, Bihar, and Orissa. Diwani rights explicitly included the right to collect revenue and administer civil justice.
○ Clive’s arrangement with Shah Alam II was inspired by practical considerations. It made the emperor a useful ‘rubber stamp’ of the Company. Besides, the emperor’s farman legalised the political gains of the Company in Bengal. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The Company **was granted with nizamat functions** (military defence, police, and administration of justice). Thus, the East India Company became the real masters of Bengal.
○ **Robert Clive introduced the dual system of government**, i.e., the rule of the two—the Company and the Nawab—in Bengal in which both the diwani, i.e., collecting revenues, and nizamat, i.e., police and judicial functions, came under the control of the Company. The Company exercised diwani rights as the diwan and the nizamat rights through its right to nominate the deputy subahdar. The nawab was responsible for maintaining peace and order, but he depended both for funds and forces upon the Company because the latter controlled the army and revenues. **So, statement III** **is correct.**
● **Robert Clive did not want to annex Awadh** because it would have placed the Company under an obligation to protect an extensive land frontier from the Afghan and the Maratha invasions. The treaty made the Nawab a firm friend of the Company and **turned Awadh into a buffer state.** **So, statement IV** **is not correct.** **Therefore option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
With reference to British administrative policies
in colonial India, which one of the following sequences is correct in terms of their introduction from earliest to latest?

options.a:
Indian Arms Act – Vernacular Press Act –
Resolution on Local Self Government – First Factory Act
options.b:
Vernacular Press Act –  Indian Arms Act –
First Factory Act – Resolution on Local Self Government
options.c:
Indian Arms Act – Vernacular Press Act –
First Factory Act – Resolution on Local Self Government
options.d:
First Factory Act – Indian Arms Act –
Vernacular Press Act – Resolution on Local Self Government

questions.explanationMarkdown:
Lord Lytton served as the Viceroy of India from 1876 to 1880. Several changes and initiatives to secure British dominance in India defined Lyttons’ interim viceroyalty. It marked the replacement of liberal imperialist policies with reactionary. The Arms Act and Vernacular Press Act were passed during his reign.
● The **Vernacular Press Act, 1878**, enacted by **Lord Lytton**, aimed to suppress **Indian-language** **newspapers** that criticized **British rule**.
○ It allowed the government to censor content, demand security deposits, and shut down newspapers without trial. ○ It targeted only vernacular press, sparking strong opposition from papers like **Somprakash and** **Amrita Bazar Patrika.**
● The **Indian Arms Act of 1878**, introduced by **Lord Lytton**, made it illegal for Indians to possess arms without a license, while exempting Europeans and colonial officials, making it a racially discriminatory law.
○ Enacted after the 1857 Revolt, it aimed to suppress nationalist resistance. ○ The Act was strongly opposed by leaders like Gandhi, Bipin Chandra Pal, and Surendranath Banerjee, and led to armed revolutionary activity and growth of secret societies like Anushilan Samiti.
● The **First Factory Act of 1881** was the **first labour law** in colonial India, introduced under pressure from **British textile industrialists**.
○ It aimed to regulate child labour by banning employment of children below 7 years, limiting their work hours to 9 per day, and ensuring monthly holidays. ○ It also mandated fencing of hazardous machinery. Though limited in scope, it marked the beginning of state intervention in industrial labour conditions.
● The **Resolution on Local Self-Government (1882)** by **Lord Ripon** aimed to promote decentralized administration by encouraging local bodies with non-official majorities and elected representatives. Though limited in powers, it laid the foundation for democratic local governance, earning Ripon the title “Father of Local Self-Government in India.” **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Many European powers, after initially establishing
a presence in India, found it difficult to sustain their empire. In this context, which one of the following was the first to withdraw their involvement from India by the mid-18th century?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The lucrative spice trade with India attracted European trading powers in India. This also led to fierce battle among them for the control of trade with the East. In this competition, the English emerged as the victorious. Other European powers left India either under agreement with English or were out rightly defeated in  battle.
● **Portuguese:** The **Portuguese** were the first to arrive in India (Vasco da Gama in 1498) and established a strong presence, particularly in Goa. **They were also the last to leave**, with Goa, Daman, and Diu being liberated by India in 1961. **So, option (a) is not correct.**
● **French:** After the Carnatic Wars in the mid-18th century, the French lost most of their significant holdings to the British, retaining only a few enclaves like **Pondicherry. These territories were integrated into** **India in 1954.** **So, option (b) is not correct.**
● **English:** The **English East India Company** was established in 1600 and set up its first permanent factory in Surat in 1613. Through a combination of trade, military strength, and political maneuvering, they gradually rose to become the dominant power. **Their rule ended with the Indian Independence Act of** **1947**, which created the independent dominions of India and Pakistan. **Option (c) is not correct.**
● **Dutch: Their power waned significantly after the** **Battle of Chinsurah (1759)** **against the British,** and their remaining factories and trading rights were gradually ceded to the British, particularly during the Napoleonic Wars and formally through treaties like the **Anglo-Dutch Treaty of 1814 (ratified 1824)**, where the Dutch finally relinquished all their claims and possessions in the Indian subcontinent to the British in exchange for Sumatra and other territories in Southeast Asia. Thus, by the early 19th century, the Dutch had effectively exited the Indian colonial scene. **Thus, they left India by the 18th century,** **therefore they were the first to leave India. So, option (d) is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Treaty Signed after
I. - Treaty of Purandhar, 1776
First Anglo-Maratha War
II. - Treaty of Seringpatnam,
Third Anglo-Mysore War
III. - Treaty of Mangalore, 1784
Second Anglo-Maratha War
IV. - Treaty of Madras, 1769
First Anglo-Mysore War
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**: Numerous **treaties** signed during the 18th and early 19th centuries, as they were fundamental **instruments of the** **British East India Company's expansionist policy** and the gradual establishment of British colonial rule in India.
● **Treaty of Purandhar (1776)** was signed between the British East India Company and the Maratha Peshwa's regent, Nana Phadnavis, **during the First Anglo-Maratha War**. **So, pair I is correct.**
● **Treaty of Seringapatam (1792)** was signed between the **British East India Company (along with its** **allies the Nizam of Hyderabad and the Maratha Empire) and Tipu Sultan, ending the Third Anglo-** **Mysore War.** Under this treaty of 1792, nearly half of Mysore's territory was taken over by the victors. **So, pairs II is correct.**
● **Treaty of Mangalore (1784)** was signed between the British East India Company and Tipu Sultan of Mysore, **ending the Second Anglo-Mysore War**. Under this each party gave back the territories it had taken from the other. **So, pair III is not correct**.
● **Treaty of Madras (1769)** was signed between the British East India Company and Hyder Ali of Mysore, **ending the First Anglo-Mysore War**. The treaty provided for the exchange of prisoners and mutual restitution of conquests. Haidar Ali was promised the help of the English in case he was attacked by any other power. **So, pair III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following statements is correct
about the Treaty of Bassein, 1802?

options.a:
The Marathas accepted the subsidiary
alliance.
options.b:
It guaranteed peace between the English and
the Marathas.
options.c:
Maratha institution of the Peshwaship was
permanently abolished.
options.d:
The British agreed to remove English troops
permanently from Maratha territory.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**: The internal feuds between Marathas provided an opportunity to Wellesley for intervention. The Peshwa put himself completely in the care of the British. Wellesley used this opportunity to oblige him to sign the humiliating Treaty of Bassein on 31 December 1802.
● **Under the treaty of Bassein Peshwa Baji Rao II signed a subsidiary alliance** and accepted the stationing of a permanent British force within his territory and ceded certain districts to the Company for the maintenance of these troops. He also agreed not to enter any alliance with other powers without British consent. **So, option (a) is correct.**
● The **Treaty of Bassein triggered the Second Anglo-Maratha War (1803-1805)**. Other powerful Maratha chiefs like Scindia and Bhonsle viewed the Peshwa's acceptance of the subsidiary alliance as a
betrayal of Maratha independence and a humiliation, leading them to wage war against the British. **Thus,** **it did not grant peace between Marathas and Britishers. So, option (b) is not correct.**
● The **Peshwaship was not abolished by the Treaty of Bassein**. Peshwa Baji Rao II remained the nominal head of the Maratha confederacy, albeit under British protection and control. The institution of the Peshwaship was finally abolished in 1818 after the Third Anglo-Maratha War. **So, option (c) is not** **correct**
● The treaty was signed by a Peshwa who lacked political authority, but the gains made by the English were immense. **The provision of keeping English troops permanently, under the policy of subsidiary** **alliance, in Maratha territory was of great strategical benefit.** The Company already had troops in Mysore, Hyderabad and Lucknow. The addition of Poona on the list meant that the Company’s troops were now more evenly spread and could be rushed to any place without much delay in times of need. **So,** **option (d) is not correct**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Who among the following established their first
factories at Musallipatnam?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Europeans arrived in India mainly for the purpose of trading. They purchased various goods from India and sold the same in Europe at high prices. To source goods locally they established trading stations known as factories initially in Southern India and then in other parts of India.
● **Dutch:** After arriving in India, the **Dutch** established their first factory at **Masulipatnam (Andhra)** in 1605. They later set up trading centers across India and became a major threat to the Portuguese.
○ The Dutch captured Nagapatam near Madras (Chennai) from the Portuguese, making it their main stronghold in South India.
● **English:** Captain Hawkins reached the court of Jahangir in April 1609 but failed to establish a factory at Surat due to Portuguese opposition, leaving Agra in 1611. In **1611**, the **English** started trading at **Masulipatnam** and set up a factory there in **1616**.
○ In 1612, Captain Thomas Best defeated the Portuguese near Surat, after which Jahangir allowed the English to set up a factory at Surat in 1613 under Thomas Aldworth. In 1615, Sir Thomas Roe came as an ambassador of James I to Jahangir’s court, staying until 1619. ○ Though he could not conclude a commercial treaty, he secured privileges for the English, including permission to establish factories at Agra, Ahmedabad, and Broach. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: Places in News and Geopolitical Hotspots (Ukraine, Taiwan Strait, Hormuz, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Governor
General
Policy adopted
I. - Warren
Hastings
The Policy of Ring
Fence
II. - Lord Wellesley Subsidiary Alliance
III. - Lord Lytton Policy of Masterly
Inactivity
IV. - John Lawrence Policy of Proud
Reserve
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The process of imperial expansion and consolidation of British paramountcy was carried on by the Company during the 1757-1857 period through a two-fold method: (a) **policy of annexation by conquest or war; and (b)** **policy of annexation by diplomacy and administrative mechanisms**. In the case of some powers, the British applied diplomatic and administrative policies.
● The **policy of Ring Fence (1765-1813)** was reflected in **Warren Hastings**’ wars against the Marathas and Mysore, and which **aimed to protect British territories by creating buffer states** around them, often involving alliances and military support to native rulers. The main threat was from the Marathas and Afghan invaders. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The **Subsidiary Alliance system** was used by **Lord Wellesley**, who was governor-general from 17981805, to build an empire in India. Wellesley’s policy of subsidiary alliance was, in fact, an extension of
the ring-fence system which sought to reduce the Indian states into a position of dependence on the British government. **So, pair II is correctly matched.**
● **John Lawrence (1864-1869) started a policy of masterly inactivity** which was a reaction to the disasters of the First Afghan War and an outcome of practical common sense and an intimate knowledge of the frontier problem and of Afghan passion for independence. **So, pair III is not correct.**
● **Lord Lytton** became the Viceroy of India in 1876. **He started a new foreign policy of ‘proud reserve**’,
which was aimed at having scientific frontiers and safeguarding ‘spheres of influence’. **So, pair IV is not** **correct.** **Therefore option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology

questions.questionText:
With reference to Indian history consider the
following pairs:
Treaty Description
I. - Treaty of Sagauli,
Nepal withdrew its territorial claims on Sikkim
II. - Treaty of Yandabo,
Provided for a permanent British Representative in Afghanistan
III. - Treaty of Gandamak,
Burma surrendered Assam to the British
IV. - Treaty of Lahore,
Sutlej was accepted as the boundary between Ranjit Singh's dominions and the East India Company
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The desire of the British imperialists to consolidate their administrative and political power in India led them into conflict with countries neighbouring India.
● **In 1801, the English annexed Gorakhpur** which brought the Gorkhas’ boundary and the Company’s boundary together. **The conflict started due to the Gorkhas’ capture of Butwal and Sheoraj** in the period of Lord Hastings (1813-23). The war ended in the **Treaty of Sagauli, 1816** which was in favour of the British. As per the treaty, Nepal accepted a British resident, Nepal ceded the districts of Garhwal and Kumaon, and abandoned claims to Terai and withdrew from Sikkim. **So, pair I is correctly matched.**
● The **Treaty of Yandabo (1826)** was **signed after the First Anglo-Burmese War** (**not the Second Anglo-** **Afghan War).** It resulted in Burma ceding territories like Assam, Manipur, and Arakan to the British, and agreeing to receive a British Resident. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● The **Treaty of Gandamak (1879)** **was signed** after the first phase of the **Second** **Anglo-Afghan War** **(not after the First Burma War)**. It involved Afghanistan ceding strategic territories and accepting British control over its foreign policy. **So, pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **The Treaty of Lahore (1846)** was signed after the First Anglo-Sikh War, following the defeat of the Sikh Empire. **This treaty restricted the Sikh Empire's power and influence, rather than extending Ranjit** **Singh's rule**. It ceded significant territory to the British, reduced the Sikh army, and essentially established British paramountcy over Punjab. The **treaty of Amritsar (1809) accepted the River Sutlej as the** **boundary line for Ranjit Singh's dominions and the Company. So, pair IV is not correctly matched.** **So, only one pair is correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which one of the statements given below is **not**
correct?

options.a:
Lord Canning introduced the portfolio system
in India.
options.b:
Lord Mayo initiated a process of financial
decentralization in India.
options.c:
Lord Ripon’s efforts led to  the establishment
of municipal bodies in India.
options.d:
Lord Minto created the North-West Frontier
Province (NWFP).

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Lord Canning,** who assumed the title of the Viceroy after the Queen’s proclamation, introduced important changes in the mode of functioning of the Council in 1859, when **he appointed a finance** **minister.** By making an individual member responsible for a particular department, Canning replaced the earlier practice of the Council deliberating each matter collectively through the exchange of minutes. The new system of granting portfolios facilitated the work of the Council and allowed the Government of India to cope with the steadily growing volume of work. Thus, **Lord Canning is responsible for the** **introducing portfolio system in India**. **As NWFP was created in 1901** and **tenure** of Lord **Canning** **ended in 1862** thus, he cannot be credited with creation of NWFP.  **So, option (a) is correct.**
● **Lord Mayo**, served as the fourth Viceroy of India from 1869 to 1872. He initiated a process of financial decentralization by granting provincial governments fixed grants for certain departments like education,
police, and jails. Provinces were then allowed to raise additional funds through local taxation to meet their expenses.  **So, option (b) is correct.**
● **Lord Ripon served as the Viceroy of India from 1880 to 1884.** Lord Ripon believed in promoting local self-government as a means of political and popular education for Indians. He passed a resolution in 1882 that led to the **establishment of municipal bodies and district boards**, with a majority of elected nonofficial members. **So, option (c) is correct.**
● **Lord Curzon, the viceroy between 1899 and 1905,** followed a **policy of withdrawal and concentration** **with respect to Afghanistan.** **He created the North-West Frontier Province (NWFP)** directly under the Government of India (earlier, it was under control of the lieutenant-governor of Punjab). He aimed to consolidate British control over the restive tribal areas bordering Afghanistan. **So, option (d) is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
the difference between Zamindars and Poligars:
I.  	 Poligars were military commanders under
the Nayaka rulers, whereas Zamindars were hereditary revenue collectors under the Mughal rule.
II. 	 Under British rule, the military powers of
Poligars were abolished, while Zamindars were formalized as revenue collectors under new land revenue systems.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Poligars**, in South India, were the holders of territory or palayam, consisting of a few villages granted to them by the rulers—mainly the Nayakas—**in** **return for military service and tribute.** They functioned under the **Nayaka rulers,** successors of the Vijayanagara Empire. **Zamindars** were **hereditary holders** **of superior rights** in land, to whom a subordinate share of revenue accrued; their participation in the agrarian system was essential not only for political reasons but also to facilitate the collection of revenue from the peasantry.
○ While Mughal-era Zamindars were often appointed and could be removed, the British institutionalized their role as **hereditary intermediaries**. ○ While Zamindars were generally **hereditary landowners** under Mughal and British administrations tasked with collecting revenue, Poligars served as **feudal chiefs or military** **commanders** who governed smaller regions and owed their loyalty to a higher sovereign authority. **So, statement I is correct.**
● After suppressing revolts like the **Poligar War (1799–1805)**, the British **abolished the military authority** of the Poligars. Meanwhile, **Zamindars retained their role** as intermediaries in land revenue administration, especially after the **Permanent Settlement of 1793**, which institutionalized their position. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● The **Zamindari system** was introduced by **Lord Cornwallis in 1793**. Cornwallis believed that the Zamindars would be more efficient at collecting revenue than the British government could be. He also believed that the Zamindars would use their wealth and influence to improve the lives of the peasants.
● The Palayakkarar (Poligar) system was a feudal military-administrative arrangement established in Tamil Nadu by **Viswanatha Nayaka** in 1529, inspired by earlier practices under **Pratapa Rudhra** **of the Kakatiya kingdom**. Under this system:
○ A **palayam** (territory or estate) was granted to individuals for **military service**. ○ There were traditionally **72 Palayakkarars**, each with autonomy to **collect taxes**, **maintain** **law and order**, and **resolve disputes**. ○ Their policing duties were known as **Padikaval** or **Arasu Kaval**.
○ They were closely allied with Nayak rulers and often **supported them in restoring** **political control**. ○ This system endured for around **two centuries**, until the **British annexed** these territories. ○ The **Poligar Revolt**, also known as the **Palayakkarars Revolt**, was a civil uprising in South India from 1799 to 1805. The term “Poligars” refers to **feudal lords** who governed fortified territories granted by the **Nayakas**. The revolt was a response to the increasing power and economic exploitation by the British East India Company.

questions.questionText:
Which of the following were the key drivers
behind the emergence of civil uprisings in 18th century India?
I. - Land revenue settlements
II. 	 Displacement of tribal communities
III.	 Collapse of indigenous industries
Select the correct answer from the codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The colonial rule profoundly impacted all aspects of Indian society. There were several civil uprisings in this period, led by the deposed rulers, ex-officials of conquered Indian states, impoverished zamindars, and feudal lords (poligars). These uprisings in most cases **represented common conditions**, though separated in time and place. The major Causes of Civil Uprisings are as follows:
● Under the Company rule, there were rapid changes in the economy, administration and land revenue system that went against the people.
● Several **zamindars and poligars** who had lost control over their **land and its revenues** due to the colonial rule, had personal scores to settle with the new rulers. **So, point I is correct.**
● The ego of traditional zamindars and poligars was hurt due to being **sidelined in rank by government** **officials** and a new class of merchants and money-lenders.
●  The **ruin of Indian handicraft industries** due to colonial policies impoverished millions of artisans whose misery was further compounded by the disappearance of their traditional patrons and buyers— princes, chieftains, and zamindars. **So, point III is correct.**
● The **priestly classes** instigated hatred and rebellion against alien rule, because the religious preachers, priests, pundits, maulvis, etc., had been dependent on the traditional landed and bureaucratic elite. The fall of zamindars and feudal lords directly affected the priestly class.
● The **foreign character of the British rulers**, who always remained alien to this land, and their contemptuous treatment of the tribal people hurt the pride of the latter. **So,  point II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● The establishment of colonial rule and its policies had a bigger annihilative effect on the Indians as a whole. There was no one to hear their grievances or pay attention to their problems. The Company was merely interested in extracting revenue.
● The colonial law and judiciary safeguarded the interest of the government and its collaborators—**the landlords, the merchants and money-lenders.** Thus, the people left with no options, chose to take up arms and defend themselves. The conditions of the tribal people were not different from those of the people living in the mainland but the encroachment by outsiders into their independent tribal polity made them **more aggrieved** **and violent.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
The Treaty of Eternal Friendship of 1809 and
the Tripartite Treaty of 1838 were signed by the British to:

options.d: Get passage to Tibet through Nepal

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● In June 1807, the alliance of Tilsit with Alexander I of Russia  was joined by Napoleon Bonaparte. The alliance had as one of its conditions a combined invasion of India by the land route. Now the British wanted to create a barrier between Russia and British India. To achieve this, **Lord Minto signed the** **Treaty of Eternal Friendship with the Amirs of Sindh.** After professing eternal friendship, both sides agreed to exclude the French from Sindh and to exchange agents at each other’s court.
● To address the Afghan problem (as the British imagined it) the East India Company **persuaded Ranjit** **Singh to sign a tripartite treaty in June 1838** **agreeing to British mediation in his disputes with the** **Amirs of Sindh**, and then made Emperor Shah Shuja give up his sovereign rights on Sindh, provided the arrears of tribute were paid. The  main objective was to obtain finances for the Afghan adventure and **obtain as much of the Amirs’ territory as would secure a line of operation against Afghanistan** **through Sindh**. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
The tribal movements of the north-eastern frontier
and non-frontier regions were different in which of the following aspects?
I. 	 The frontier tribal revolts under the British
continued for a longer time than the nonfrontier tribal movements.
II. 	 Frontier tribal movements were not forest-
based or agrarian revolts.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
When the British implemented new rules in tribal areas the boundary between the tribals and the state was broken. This led to many clashes between the Government and the tribal people.   The movements of the tribes of the **north-eastern frontier were different from the non-frontier tribal revolts** in some aspects.
● The tribes in the frontier region which shared tribal and cultural links with countries across the border did not concern themselves much with the nationalist struggle. Their revolts were often in favour of political autonomy within the Indian Union or complete independence. **These movements were not forest-based** **or agrarian revolts** as these tribals were generally in control of land and forest area. **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● The British entered the north-eastern areas much later than the non-frontier tribal areas.
● **The frontier tribal revolts under the British continued for a longer time than the non-frontier tribal** **movements**. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
**Common characteristics of the frontier and non-frontier tribal uprisings:**
● **Tribal identity or ethnic ties** **lay behind the solidarity** shown by these groups. Not all ‘outsiders’ were, however, seen as enemies: the poor who lived by their manual labour or profession and had a socially/economically supportive role in the village were left alone; the violence was directed towards the money-lenders and traders who were seen as extensions of the colonial government.
● A common cause was the resentment against the **imposition of laws by the ‘foreign** **government’** that was seen as an effort at destroying the tribals’ traditional socioeconomic framework.
● Many uprisings were led by messiah-like figures who **encouraged their people to revolt** and who held out the promise that they could end their suffering brought about by the ‘outsiders’.
● The tribal uprisings were doomed from the beginning, given the **outdated arms** they fought with as against the modern weapons and techniques used by their opponents.
**Relevance**: Janjatiya Gaurav Divas was recently celebrated marking the birth anniversary of Bhagwan Birsa Munda, a tribal leader and freedom fighter.

questions.questionText:
Consider the following information:
Revolt/ Uprising
Leader(s) Region
I. - Ho tribal uprisings
Raja Parahat
Assam
II. - Kondh uprisings
Chakra Bisoi
Eastern Ghats
III. - Koya revolts Sidhu and Kanhu
Andhra Pradesh In how many of the above rows is the given information correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Tribal movements in India during British rule were marked by their frequent, militant, and violent nature.
● **Ho Uprising (1820-1837)**: **The Raja of Parahat** organised his Ho tribals to revolt against the occupation of **Singhbhum (now in Jharkhand).** The revolt continued till 1827 when the Ho tribals were forced to submit. However, later in 1831, they again organised a rebellion, **joined by the Mundas of** **Chhotanagpur**, to protest against the newly introduced farming revenue policy and the entry of Bengalis into their region. **So, row I is not correctly matched.**
● **Khond Uprisings (1837-1856):** From 1837 to 1856, the Khonds of the hilly tracts extending from **Odisha** **to the Srikakulam and Visakhapatnam districts of Andhra Pradesh** **(areas around Eastern Ghats)** revolted against Company rule. **Chakra Bisoi**, a young raja, led the Khonds who were joined by the Ghumsar, Kalahandi and other tribals to oppose the suppression of human sacrifice, new taxes, and the entry of zamindars into their areas. **So, row II is correctly matched.**
● **Koya Rebellion (1879-80):** It took place in **the eastern Godavari tract of Andhra Pradesh and some** **regions of Malkangiri region in Odisha**. It was led by **Tomma Dora**, the Koya leader**.** The reasons for the rebellion include erosion of customary rights over forests, mansabdars effort to enhance taxes on timber and grazing, police exactions & exploitations by money lenders, new excise regulation restricting domestic production of toddy, etc. **So, row III is not correctly matched.** **So, only two of the above rows are correctly matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology

questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Disintegration of the older system of zamindari
II. 	 High tariffs on Indian-made goods in Britain
III. 	The Religious Disabilities Act of 1856
How many of the above reasons contributed to the Revolt of 1857?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The causes of the revolt of 1857, like those of earlier uprisings, emerged from all aspects—**socio-cultural,** **economic and political**—of daily existence of the Indian population cutting through all sections and classes.
● **Zamindars,** the traditional landed aristocracy, often saw their land rights forfeited with frequent use of a quo warranto by the administration. This resulted in a **loss of status** for them in the villages. Impoverished by **heavy taxation**, the peasants resorted to loans from money-lenders/traders at usurious rates, the latter often evicting the former from their land on non-payment of debt dues. These money-lenders and traders emerged as the **new landlords**, while the scourge of landless peasantry and rural indebtedness has continued to plague Indian society to this day. The older system of **zamindari** was forced to **disintegrate**. **So, point I is correct.**
● The Indian trade and mercantile class were deliberately crippled by the British who imposed **high tariff duties** **on Indian-made goods**. At the same time, **the import of British goods into India attracted low tariffs**, thus encouraging their entry into India. By the mid-nineteenth century, **exports of cotton and silk textiles** from India practically came to an **end**. One way free trade and refusal to impose protective duties against machine-made goods from Britain simply killed Indian manufacture. **So, point II is correct.**
o It was the British intruder who broke up the Indian handloom and destroyed the spinning-wheel. England began with depriving the Indian cottons from the European market; it then introduced a twist into Hindustan and in the end inundated the very mother country of cotton with cottons.” —**Karl Marx, 1853**
● **The Religious Disabilities Act, 1856** modified Hindu customs and declared that a change of religion did not debar a son from inheriting the property of his ‘heathen’ father. The fear of interference in customs was further intensified by **the government’s decision to tax mosque and temple lands.** **So, point III is correct.** **So, only three of the above reasons contributed to the Revolt of 1857.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Advent of Europeans, British Expansion, Anglo Wars
questions.questionText:
Consider the following :
I. 	 Protection of the rights of the native princes
II. 	 Freedom of religion
III. 	Equal opportunities for Indians in government
services
IV. 	 Progressive reforms in economy
How many of the following reforms were promised for India by the ‘Queen’s Proclamation’ issued in 1858?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
In 1858 the British Parliament passed an **Act for the Better Government of India**. Through this Act the direct responsibility for the administration of the country was assumed by the British Crown and Company rule was abolished. The assumption of the Government of India by the sovereign of Great Britain was announced after the 1857 revolt by **Lord Canning** at a durbar at Allahabad in **the ‘Queen’s Proclamation’** issued on November 1, 1858.
● **Promise to respect the rights of the native princes:** As per the Queen’s proclamation, the era of annexations and expansion had ended and the British promised to **respect the dignity and rights of the native princes**. However, the Indian states were henceforth to recognize the paramountcy of the British Crown. **So, point I** **is correct.**
● **Freedom of religion:** The people of India were promised **freedom of religion without interference from** **British officials**. It was also promised that old Indian rights, customs and practices would be given due regard while framing and administering the law. As such, the British effort to bring about reforms via legislature in the religion and customs of Indians came to an end. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Equal opportunities in government services:** The Queen's Proclamation of 1858 promised that **appointments would be made without discrimination based on race or creed**. The Queen Victoria’s 1858 promise of racial equality of opportunity in the selection of civil servants for the government of India had **theoretically thrown the ICS open to qualified Indians, but examinations for the services were given** **only in Britain** and only to male applicants between the ages of 17 and 22 (in 1878 the maximum age was further reduced to 19) who could stay in the saddle over a rigorous series of hurdles. **So, point III is correct**.
● **Progressive reforms in economy:** The Proclamation **did not explicitly promise any type progressive** **economic reforms**. The Act of 1858 and Queen’s Proclamation were focused on polity and administration in India. However, it laid the groundwork for potential future economic changes by shifting governance to the British Crown. **So, point IV is not correct.** **So, only three of the above statements are correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● The Act declared **Queen Victoria** as the sovereign of British India and provided for the appointment of a **Secretary of State for India** (a member of the British cabinet).
● The direct responsibility for the administration of the country was assumed by the British Crown and Company rule was abolished.
● The **Army**, which was at the forefront of the outbreak, was thoroughly reorganised and British military policy came to be dominated by the idea of “**division and counterpoise**”. The British could no longer depend on Indian loyalty, so the **number of Indian soldiers was drastically** **reduced** even as the number of European soldiers was increased. The concept of **divide and** **rule** was adopted with separate units being created on the basis of caste/community/region.
Recruits were to be drawn from the ‘**martial’ races** of Punjab, Nepal, and north-western frontier who had proved loyal to the British during the Revolt. Effort was made to keep the army away from civilian population.
● **The Army Amalgamation Scheme, 1861** moved the Company’s European troops to the services of the Crown. Further, the European troops in India were constantly revamped by periodical visits to England, sometimes termed as the **‘linked-battalion’ scheme.** All Indian artillery units, except a few mountain batteries, were made defunct. All higher posts in the army and the artillery departments were reserved for the Europeans.
● The earlier reformist zeal of a self-confident Victorian belief that Indians were beyond reform. This new approach— **‘conservative brand of liberalism’, as it was called by Thomas** **Metcalf—**had the solid support of the conservative and aristocratic classes of England who espoused the complete non-interference in the traditional structure of Indian society. Thus, the era of reforms came to an end.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Advent of Europeans, British Expansion, Anglo Wars
questions.questionText:
During the British rule in India, the policy of
“division and counterpoise” was applied for:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Revolt of 1857, also known as **the "First War of Independence,"** was the first significant attempt by Indians to end British imperialism. It started on **10 May 1857**, in the form of sepoy mutiny. Since, military was at the forefront of revolt, the British employed various strategies to maintain firm grip over the military.
● The Army, which was at the forefront of the outbreak, was thoroughly reorganized and **British military** policy came to be dominated by the idea of **“division and counterpoise”**. The British could no longer depend on Indian loyalty, so the number of Indian soldiers was drastically reduced even as the number of European soldiers was increased. Earlier, the number of Indian soldiers (at lower rank) was significantly higher when compared to Europeans.
● The concept of divide and rule was adopted with separate units being created on the basis of caste/community/region. Recruits were to be drawn from the ‘martial’ races of Punjab, Nepal, and northwestern frontier who had proved loyal to the British during the Revolt. Efforts were made to keep the army away from the civilian population. Prior to the revolt, there was no concept of martial and non-martial races. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Advent of Europeans, British Expansion, Anglo Wars
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Revolt of 1857 helped to give rise to modern nationalism by awakening political consciousness among Indians.
**Statement II:** The Revolt of 1857 established local traditions of resistance to British rule by fostering a sense of collective identity.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and statement II is the correct explanation for Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II is **not** the correct explanation for Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Revolt of 1857, also known as **the "First War of Independence,"** was the first significant attempt by Indians to end British imperialism. For the British, the Revolt of 1857 proved useful in that it showed the **glaring** **shortcomings in the Company’s administration** and its army, which they rectified promptly. These defects would never have been revealed to the world if the Revolt had not happened.
● The conservative reaction in England made the British Empire in India more autocratic; it began to deny the aspirations of the educated Indians for sharing power. In the long term, this new British attitude proved **counter-productive for the Empire**, as this caused frustrations in the educated Indian middle classes and gave **rise to modern nationalism** very soon. **So, Statement-I is correct.**
● The Revolt of 1857, while suppressed, did indeed **establish local traditions of resistance to British rule** **by fostering a sense of collective identity** and inspiring future generations to fight for independence. The revolt demonstrated that Indians were willing to challenge the British Empire, and it laid the groundwork for a more organized and sustained freedom movement.  **So, while Statement-II is correct, it also explains** **Statement-I.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is the correct explanation for Statement**
**I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Development of Press, Education
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with reference
to social reformers in 19th century India:
I. 	 They prioritized modern knowledge over
traditional knowledge through promotion of science, reason and innovation.
II. 	 They advocated for use and development
of native Indian languages over foreign languages.
III. 	They demanded creation of an alternative
system of education with emphasis on vocational education.
IV. 	 They attempted to replicate western scientific
medicines.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The dawn of the nineteenth century witnessed the birth of a new vision—a modern vision among some enlightened sections of Indian society. The evolution of an alternative cultural-ideological system and the regeneration of traditional institutions were two concerns of these movements.
● **Raja Ram Mohan Roy** used Hindu scriptures (Upanishads) to argue against sati and for monotheism. Similarly, **Swami Dayananda Saraswati** emphasized going back to the Vedas because he believed that the original Vedic religion was pure and rational. **They did not prioritize modern knowledge over** **traditional one, rather used the former to make the latter look reasonable and just**. **So, statement I** **is not correct**.
● The 19th century Indian social reformers actively used and promoted **native Indian languages** as tools for **social, religious, and educational reform**.  **So, statement II is correct.** o Social reformers wrote books, poems, and newspapers in vernacular languages like Bengali, Hindi, Marathi, etc., to reach ordinary people. For instance, Phule wrote “Gulamgiri” in Marathi to attack caste discrimination. o Newspapers and journals were published in native languages to spread social and political awareness. For example, Tilak’s Kesari was published in Marathi and Harishchandra produced journals in Hindi.
● Several **Indian social reformers of the 19th century** emphasized the **creation of an alternative system of** **education**, particularly focusing on **vocational and practical education**, as a response to colonial education policies that largely prioritized rote learning and produced clerks for British administration. **So, statement III** **is correct.**
o **Jyotirao Phule**  emphasis on practical knowledge and his schools included vocational subjects, not just religious or literary studies. o **Rabindranath Tagore** believed that education should be in natural surroundings, encouraging creativity and practical skills. This University emphasized a holistic education combining Indian traditional knowledge.
● The 19th-century Indian social reformers **made significant efforts to revitalize Indian systems of medicine** **such as Ayurveda, Unani, and Siddha**, often in response to the growing dominance of Western medicine under British colonial rule. For instance, Pandit Madan Mohan Malaviya helped found **Banaras Hindu** **University (BHU), where Ayurveda was taught alongside modern medicine**. Similarly, **Pandit Gopi** **Chand Sen in Bengal published Ayurvedic texts** and argued for the scientific nature of Ayurveda. **So,** **statement IV is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Social reformer Contribution
I. - Jagannath Shankar Seth
Native School of Bombay
II. - Vishnu Shastri Pandit
Students' Literary and Scientific Society
III. - B.M. Malabari Notes on Infant Marriage and Enforced Widowhood
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
On the questions of women, the 19th century reformers appealed to **individualism** and **equality**, arguing that true religion did not sanction women’s inferior status. They opposed **polygamy, purdah, child marriage**, and restrictions on **widow remarriage**, while working to promote **women’s education** and press for **favourable** **legislation**..
● **Jagannath Shankar Sheth** became an active leader in many arenas of life in Mumbai. Foreseeing the need for improvements in education, he became one of the founders of the **School Society and the Native** **School of Bombay**, the first of its kind in Western India.
o When the Students' Literary and Scientific Society **first opened their girls' schools, Jagannath** **Shankarsheth contributed much of the necessary funds**, despite strong opposition of some members of the Hindu community. **So, pair I is correct.**
● **Vishnu Shastri Pandit** played a **key role in promoting widow remarriage** in 19th-century India, particularly in **Maharashtra**. His contributions were part of the larger social reform movement that sought to challenge orthodox Hindu practices and uplift the status of women.
o He worked closely with other reformers like **Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar**, who was active in Bengal, and supported efforts that led to the passing of the **Hindu Widows' Remarriage Act of** **1856**. This act legally permitted Hindu widows to remarry. **So, pair II is correct.**
● **Behramji Merwanji Malabari** was a prominent social reformer who actively campaigned against child marriage and other social evils in 19th-century India.
o **“Notes on Infant Marriage and Enforced Widowhood” (1884):** This was his most famous publication, in which he strongly criticized child marriage and the custom of preventing widows from remarrying. The pamphlet gained wide attention in both India and Britain and influenced public opinion. o His advocacy helped create momentum for the Age of Consent Act of 1891, which raised the minimum age of consent for girls from 10 to 12 years in British India. **So, pair III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Consider the following factors:
I. 	 Creation of private property in land
II. 	 Introduction of railways
III. 	Uniform legal system based on written laws
Which of the above factors led to the weakening of the caste structure during colonial India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **British creation of private property in land** — especially through the Permanent Settlement (1793), Ryotwari, and Mahalwari — weakened its foundations of caste structure. This **monetized relationships** **that were earlier caste-bound** and based on mutual obligations, reducing caste-based occupational interdependence. **Private property allowed economically mobile lower castes (like some Shudra** **communities) to acquire land, breaking traditional caste restrictions**. **So, point I is correct.**
● In traditional society, strict rules governed inter-caste distance, dining, and travel. But **railway** **compartments forced people of different castes to sit together, often in close quarters, breaking** **social taboos**. Railways increased physical mobility, allowing people from lower castes to travel for work, education, and opportunities. This helped **break village-level caste hierarchies** and enabled social and economic mobility. **So, point II is correct.**
● The **new system of justice** introduced by the British in India during the 19th century replaced customary, caste-based justice with a uniform legal system based on written **laws**, which **undermined the authority** **of caste panchayats and Brahminical legal traditions**. Now, lower castes could approach colonial courts to challenge discriminatory practices, land disputes, or caste-based oppression. **The new legal system** **promoted the idea of the individual as the legal subject, rather than the caste collective.** This shift
undermined the idea of inherited caste-based duties and allowed people to assert legal rights as citizens. **So, point III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 He started India’s first school for widows.
II. 	 He established India’s first university for
women.
III. 	He established the widow Marriage
Association.
Who was this personality?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Dr Dhondo Keshav Karve,** is considered as a pioneer for women empowerment in India. He established the **Widow Marriage Association in 1893** and in the same year, he shocked everyone after he opted to marry a widow himself. His first wife had passed away earlier in the year 1891.
● Continuing with his effort for helping widows, Maharshi Karve even founded an educational institution, Hindu Widows Home, in 1896, in Poona to help widows support themselves, in case they were unable to remarry.
● After his retirement from Fergusson College, Poona, where he worked as maths instructor, he started the **Shreemati Nathibai Damodar Thackersey Women University in 1916**.  He also worked towards abolishing the caste system and established societies for village primary education. **Karve was inspired** **by the works of Ishwar Chandra Vidyasagar, Pandita Ramabai, and Herbert Spencer.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following:
I. 	 Universal message of Vedanta
II. 	 All humans are manifestations of the Divine
III. 	Fundamental distinction among western and
eastern religions
How many of the above were the basic tenets of
the Ramakrishna Movement?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Ramakrishna Movement was initiated by Swami Vivekananda to spread the universal message of Vedanta in the light of Sri Ramakrishna and to work for the alleviating of the poor and the downtrodden with the motto **‘for** **one’s own liberation and for the good of the world’**. Two objectives of the Ramakrishna movement were:
● **First:** To bring into existence a band of monks dedicated to a life of renunciation and practical spirituality, from among whom teachers and workers would be sent out **to spread the universal message of Vedanta** as illustrated in the life of Ramakrishna. **So, point I is correct**.
● **Second:** In conjunction with lay disciples to carry on preaching, philanthropic and charitable works, **looking upon all men, women and children, irrespective of caste, creed or colour, as veritable** **manifestations of the Divine.** **So, point II is correct.** o Paramahamsa himself laid the foundations of the Ramakrishna Math with his young **monastic** **disciples** as a nucleus to fulfil the first objective. The **second objective was taken up by Swami** **Vivekananda** after Ramakrishna’s death when he founded the Ramakrishna Mission in 1897.
● Paramahamsa sought salvation through traditional ways of renunciation, meditation and bhakti amidst increasing westernisation and modernisation. He **recognised the fundamental oneness of all religions** and emphasised that Krishna, Hari, Ram, Christ, Allah are different names for the same God, and that there are many ways to God and salvation. As many faiths, so many paths. **So, point III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box:**
● **Ramakrishna Paramahamsa (1836–1886)**, born as **Gadadhar Chattopadhyay**, was a poor priest at the **Kali temple in Dakshineshwar**, near Calcutta.
● While the **didactic nationalism of the Brahmo Samaj** appealed to Bengal’s intellectual elite, the **bhakti and yoga-based teachings** of Ramakrishna attracted the common people.
● From an early age, he experienced **spiritual trances** and is regarded as having attained the **highest spiritual experience** in Hinduism.
● He did not write books; his **simple conversations**, rich in **parables and metaphors**, formed the core of his teachings.
● His wife, **Sarada Devi**, revered as the **Holy Mother**, played a key role in supporting his disciples and mission.

questions.questionText:
Which of the following statements are correct
with regard to the Theosophical Society?
I. 	 It was founded in New York City in 1875 and
later on shifted its headquarters to Adyar in
India.
II. 	 It accepted the Hindu beliefs in reincarnation
and karma.
III. 	Annie Besant was elected as the first women
president of the Society in 1907.
Select the correct answer from the code given
below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **The Theosophical Society was founded in New York City in 1875 by Helena Blavatsky**, a Russian mystic, and Henry Steel Olcott, the society’s first president. The society seeks to bridge the divide between East and West by emphasising the similarities in human culture. In India, the Theosophical society played a key role in the Hindu awakening by promoting literary and research activities, reviving faith in Hindu philosophies, studying Aryan religion, publishing and translating scriptures, and supporting reforms and education. **In 1882,** **they shifted their headquarters to Adyar**, on the outskirts of Madras (at that time) in India. **So, statement** **I is correct**
● The society believed that a special relationship could be established between a person’s soul and God by contemplation, prayer, revelation, etc. **It accepted the Hindu beliefs in reincarnation and karma**, and drew inspiration from the philosophy of the Upanishads and samkhya, yoga and Vedanta schools of thought. **As** **such, the society also sought to investigate the unexplained laws of nature and the powers latent in man**. **So, statement II is correct**.
● In India, the movement became somewhat popular with the **election of Annie Besant (1847-1933) as its** **president after the death of Olcott in 1907.** Annie Besant had come to India in 1893. She laid the foundation of the Central Hindu College in Benaras in 1898 where both Hindu religion and Western scientific subjects were taught. The college became the nucleus for the formation of Banaras Hindu University in 1916. **So,** **statement III is correct**. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Socio-Religious Reform Movements
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** Social reform movements in the 19th century helped to end India’s cultural and intellectual isolation from the rest of the world.
**Statement II:** Social reformers in India tried to integrate modern ideas and culture into Indian cultural streams.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II is the correct explanation for Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II is **not** the correct explanation for Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement  II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **socio-religious reform movements** gave the rising middle classes the much needed cultural roots to cling to, and served the purpose of reducing the sense of humiliation which the conquest by a foreign power had produced.
● Although traditional values and customs were a prominent target of attack from the reformers, yet the reformers aimed at modernization rather than outright westernization based on blind imitation of alien Western cultural values. o In fact, the reform movements sought to create a favourable social climate for modernization. To that extent, **these movements ended India’s cultural and intellectual isolation from the rest of the world**. **So, Statement I is correct**.
● The underlying concern of these reformist efforts was revival of the native cultural personality which had got distorted by various factors over the years. As such, **the reformers tried to integrate modern ideas and** **culture into Indian cultural streams**. Outright rejection of traditions and customs will have led to reaction from the society which was quite evident in the grievances of people against British rule. o Reformers like Raja Ram Mohan Roy, Swami Vivekananda, and Dayanand Saraswati worked to reinterpret ancient Indian texts to show the rational and spiritual strength of Indian traditions. This was an effort to **reclaim cultural pride and show that Indian civilization had its own philosophical and moral** **foundations**. **So, Statement II is correct and it also explains Statement I.**
● **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is the correct explanation for** **Statement** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Which one of the following movements/
organisations started the ‘Pledge Movement’in India in the late 19th century?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Pledge Movement** was a significant part of the **Indian social reform movement in the late 19th century**, specifically aimed at **promoting widow remarriage** and **discouraging child marriage** and other regressive social customs. **Indian Social Conference**, founded by M.G. Ranade and Raghunath Rao, advocated inter-caste marriages, opposed polygamy and kulinism. It launched the ‘Pledge Movement’ to inspire people to take a pledge against child marriage. In this movement, **people were encouraged to take a public pledge** that they would **not** **marry off their daughters at a very young age** and that they would **support widow remarriage** within their communities. **So, option (d) is correct**.
● Objectives of the Pledge Movement: o **Discourage Child Marriage:** The movement urged families not to marry off girls before a certain age, aligning with the growing call for raising the age of consent. o **Support Widow Remarriage:** Participants pledged to promote or allow widow remarriage, challenging orthodox Hindu social practices. o **Create Moral Pressure:** By making it a public declaration, it aimed to create social accountability and spread the reformist message more effectively. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**
