questions.questionText:
In context of history of India, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 The East India Association was founded
in 1843 to safeguard the interests of the landlords.
II. 	 The Indian League was established by Sisir
Kumar Ghosh to promote political education in India.
Which of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Early nineteenth-century associations were **local**, dominated by **wealthy aristocrats**, and sought **administrative** **reforms** through petitions.  Later, **educated middle class** professionals led political associations with a **wider** **perspective** and **larger agenda** in the second half of the century.
● The **East India Association** was organised by Dadabhai Naoroji in **1866** in London to discuss the Indian question and influence public men in England to promote Indian welfare. Later, branches of the association were started in prominent Indian cities. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The **Indian League** was started in **1875** by Sisir Kumar Ghosh with the object of “stimulating the sense of nationalism amongst the people” and of encouraging political education. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
The main purpose of founding the Poona
Sarvajanik Sabha in Bombay was to:

options.a:
Regulate the age of marriage and promote
education for girls
options.b: Abolition of salt duty and stamp duty
options.c:
Address public grievances to the British
government
options.d:
Demand better wages and working conditions
for workers

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Poona Sarvajanik Sabha** was one of the most influential political organisations in 19th-century India. It  was founded in 1867 by Mahadev Govind Ranade with the assistance of his associates, Ganesh Vasudeo Joshi, S.H. Sathe, S.H. Chiplonkar, and others.
● The Poona Sarvajanik Sabha contributed significantly to the early nationalist movement by advocating for civil rights and social reforms and increasing Indian participation in governance.
● The Sabha was **formed in response to public dissatisfaction with local governance, and to mediate** **between the government and the people**. As such, it was one of the first Indian socio-political organisations **formed to represent the interests of the Indian people under British rule**.
● Poona Sarvajanik Sabha began with the goal of **serving as a mediator between the government and the** **people of India and popularising peasants’ legal rights**. It was formed due to the need for a modern socio-political organisation capable of effectively representing the interests of the Indian people. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Where was the first session of the Indian National
Congress organised in December 1885?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
In the later 1870s and early 1880s, a solid ground had been prepared for the establishment of an all-India organisation. As a prelude to an all India effort, two sessions of the Indian National Conference had been held in 1883 and 1885, which had representatives drawn from all major towns of India.
● The final shape of the idea of an all India organisation was given by a retired English civil servant, A.O. Hume, who mobilised leading intellectuals of the time and, with their cooperation, **organised the first** **session of the Indian National Congress at Gokuldas Tejpal Sanskrit College in Bombay in** **December 1885**. Hereafter, the Congress met every year in December, in a different part of the country each time. The first session of the Indian National Congress was attended by **72 delegates** and presided over by **Womesh Chandra Bonnerjee**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regards to
the Indian National Conference, a predecessor to the Indian National Congress:
I. 	 It was founded in 1883 in Kolkata by
Surendranath Banerjee along with Anand Mohan Bose.
II. 	 Its objective was to bring together political
leaders from different regions of India to discuss and express national grievances against British policies.
III. 	Its first session was prompted by the
introduction of the Vernacular Press Act in 1878.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Indian National Conference** was an early political organization in India that preceded the formation of the Indian National Congress. It played an important role in laying the groundwork for an all-India political platform. It was founded in **1883 in Kolkata by Surendranath Banerjee along with Anand Mohan Bose**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The **objective** of the Indian National Conference was to **bring together political leaders from different** **regions of India to discuss and express national grievances against British policies.** It is also aimed at promoting unity among educated Indians, demand greater Indian participation in the legislative councils and civil services and build a national political consciousness. **So, statement II is correct.**
● The first Indian National Conference was organized by the Indian Association in Kolkata between December 28 and 30, 1883. It was presided over by Ramtanu Lahiri, a veteran of the Bengal Renaissance. **The** **immediate reason was the introduction of the Criminal Procedure Amendment Bill (1883-1884), or the** **Ilbert Bill.** Ananda Mohan Bose mentioned this conference as the first step towards a national Indian Parliament. The session demanded representative assemblies of the people of India. **So, statement III is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
In a paper 'England's Debt to India', he put forward
a theory to explain British exploitation of India and transforming a self-sufficient Indian economy into a colonial economy. He was:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: c** **Explanation**: The early nationalists like Dadabhai Naoroji, R.C. Dutt, and Dinshaw Wacha carefully analysed the political economy of British rule in India, and put forward the “drain theory” to explain British exploitation of India. They opposed the transformation of a basically self-sufficient Indian economy into a colonial economy.
● The first expression of the drain theory by **Dadabhai Naoroji** was through his paper titled **‘England’s Debt** **to India’**, which he presented at the gathering of the East India Association in London (1867). He argues that **India was continuously bled and compelled to pay an indirect tribute to England.** He urged Britain to ensure equitable financial relations and suggested using foreign capital to enhance the Indian economy.
● Other nationalists like M.G. Ranade, Bholonath Chandra, and R C Dutt also criticized the imperialists for draining wealth from India. The national media soon took up the concept of ‘drain’, which eventually culminated in adopting the theory by the Indian National Congress in 1896, which attributed the famines and poverty in India to the drain of wealth. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Early National Movement - Moderates, Extremists
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
I. 	 British rule was fundamentally just, but
misinformed about the true conditions in
India.
II. 	 India’s interests lie in severing political
connections with Britain.
III. 	The British government would agree to ‘Self-
Rule’ for India if  it was properly addressed to
them.
How many of the above were assumptions of early
Moderates regarding the British rule in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Most leaders of the Indian National Congress during the 1885-1905 phase were staunch believers in ‘liberalism’ and ‘moderate’ politics. They came to be labelled Moderates to distinguish them from the neo-nationalists of the early 20th century, who were referred to as the Extremists.
● The Moderates believed that the **British basically wanted to be just to the Indians, but were not aware of** **the real conditions**. Therefore, if public opinion could be created in the country and public demands be presented to the government through resolutions, petitions, meetings, etc., the authorities would concede these demands gradually. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Moderate leaders believed that **political connections with Britain were in India’s interest** at that stage of history and that the time was not ripe for a direct challenge to British rule. They expected the British to develop institutions and governance mechanisms that will help India in the long-term. **So, statement II is not** **correct.**
● **In 1905, the Congress formally put forth the demand for Swaraj** or self-rule for Indians within the British Empire on the model of the self-governing colonies like Australia or Canada. To achieve these ends, moderates worked on a two-pronged methodology—one, **create a strong public opinion to arouse** **consciousness and national spirit and then educate and unite people on common political questions**; and two, **persuade the British Government and British public opinion to introduce reforms** in India on the lines laid out by the nationalists. **So, statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement-I:**
The British Government justified the partition of
Bengal to improve administrative efficiency.
**Statement-II:**
The eastern Bengal had remained impoverished
and undeveloped because of its isolation and poor
communications.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of
the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II is the correct explanation for
Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II is **not** the correct explanation
for Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**: The British government’s decision to partition Bengal had been made public in December 1903. The idea was to have two provinces: Bengal comprising Western Bengal as well as the provinces of Bihar and Orissa, and Eastern Bengal and Assam. Bengal retained Calcutta as its capital, while Dacca became the capital of Eastern Bengal.
● The British reasoned that Bengal was a vast province with a population of 78 million, and the British believed that its **division into two smaller provinces, East Bengal and Assam, and West Bengal, would lead to** **better governance and more effective control**. It was also stated that partition would help in the development of Assam if it came under the direct jurisdiction of the government. **Thus, the partition of** **Bengal was to improve administrative efficiency. So statement I is correct.**
● The politically and economically significant Bengal Presidency included Bihar (including the now-separate state of Jharkhand) and Orissa (now Odisha) and had formed a single province of British India since 1765. By 1900 the province had grown too large to handle under a single administration. **Eastern Bengal, because**
**of isolation and poor communications, had been neglected in favor of western Bengal and Bihar**. Commercial activity and modernization largely centered on Calcutta (now Kolkata), which was the capital of both the presidency and British India. In comparison, **the eastern region was impoverished and** **undeveloped.** (However, the real motive behind the partition plan was the British desire to weaken Bengal, the nerve centre of Indian nationalism, through its partition along communal lines). **So, Statement-II is** **correct and also explains Statement-I** **So, both Statement–I and Statement–II are correct and Statement–II is the correct explanation for** **Statement–I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Insurance and Pension
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
National Song ‘Vande Mataram’:
I. 	 It is a poem written by  Bankim Chandra
Chatterjee in his book Anandmath.
II. 	 It is composed in Sanskrit but written in the
Bengali script.
III. 	It became the theme song of the Swadeshi
movement.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: d** **Explanation**:
● **Vande Mataram, poem by Bengali writer Bankim Chandra Chatterjee** in his novel Anandamath (1882). Part of the poem, greatly regarded for its nationalist vision of India as a goddess, was set to music and became the foremost of Indian patriotic songs. It was officially adopted as the national song on January 24, 1950 (“Jana Gana Mana” is the national anthem) when independent India became a republic in 1950. **So, statement** **I is correct.**
● **“Vande Mataram,” composed in Sanskrit but written in the Bengali script,** appeared as a devotional poem of six stanzas in Anandamath, a novel set amid the sannyasi rebellion (an early anti-colonial uprising by Hindu ascetics against the East India Company’s rule in India) and a famine that devastated Bengal in 1770. **So, statement II is correct.**
● **On October 16, 1905, the day the Bengal partition formally came into force,** was observed as a day of mourning throughout Bengal. People fasted, bathed in the Ganga, and **walked barefoot in processions** **singing Vande Mataram, which almost spontaneously became the theme song of the movement. So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
“Political freedom is the life breath of a nation”
was the famous statement given by Aurobindo Ghose at the time of :
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Aurobindo Ghosh wrote a series of articles that first appeared in the daily** **Bande Mataram** **under the** **general title of New Thought from April 11 to April 23, 1907. This was the peak of the Swadeshi Movement.** In a series of articles, published in this paper soon after the Calcutta session of the Congress, Aurobindo Ghosh sought to indicate his view on both of the ideal which the Congress had adopted, the ideal of Swaraj or SelfGovernment as it exists in the United Kingdom or the Colonies, and of the possible lines of policy by which that ideal might be attained.
● There are, Ghosh pointed out, only three possible policies: petitioning, an unprecedented way of attempting a nation's liberty, which cannot possibly succeed except under conditions which have not yet existed among human beings; self-development and self-help; and the old orthodox historical method of organised resistance to the existing form of Government.
● We acknowledge that the policy of self-development which the New Party had forced to the front, was itself a novel departure under the circumstances of modern India.
● Self-development of an independent nation is one thing; self-development from a state of servitude under an alien and despotic rule without the forcible or peaceful removal of that rule as an indispensable preliminary, is quite another.
● No national self-development is possible without the support of rāja-śakti, organised political strength, commanding, and whenever necessary compelling general allegiance and obedience.
● **Political freedom is the life-breath of a nation**; to attempt social reform, educational reform, industrial expansion, the moral improvement of the race without aiming first and foremost at political freedom, is the very height of ignorance and futility.
● Such attempts are foredoomed to disappointment and failure; yet when the disappointment and failure come, we choose to attribute them to some radical defect in the national character; as if the nation were at fault and not its wise men who would not or could not understand the first elementary conditions of success.
● The primary requisite for national progress, national reform, is the free habit of free and healthy national thought and action which is impossible in a state of servitude. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Geography
questions.microTopic: Physiography of India (Location, Division, Physiographic Regions)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The Swadeshi Movement failed to reach the masses on a wide scale.
**Statement II:** The Swadeshi Movement was largely led by educated middle-class elites and lacked mass organizations, trade unions, or peasant bodies.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II is the correct explanation for Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II is **not** the correct explanation for Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The year 1905 is a watershed in Indian history. The entire scenario of the Swadeshi movement is ascribed to the Partition of Bengal. By 1908, the open phase of the Swadeshi movement was over.
● The **Swadeshi Movement (1905–1911)** was a **landmark nationalist movement**, but it did **fail to reach** **the masses on a wide scale**, especially in rural areas. Despite its energetic beginning and significant impact in Bengal and some urban centers, its influence remained **limited in scope and duration**. **Reasons:**
○ **Urban and Elite Focus:** The movement was largely led by educated middle-class elites, students, lawyers, and professionals in urban areas. The rural population, which made up the majority of India, was not deeply involved due to lack of access, awareness, or ideological connection. ○ **Absence of Grassroots Organization:** There was no structured or sustained rural mobilization. The movement lacked mass organizations, trade unions, or peasant bodies that could connect with everyday issues of the poor. ○ **Lack of Communal Unity:** The movement was strongest among Hindus in Bengal, but did not unite different religious and social communities effectively. ○ **Limited Geographical Spread:** The movement was **centered mainly in Bengal, while large** **parts of India remained untouched or only peripherally affected**. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **The Swadeshi movement was able to include within its ambit the unpoliticized section of society,** **like women who were traditionally homebound**. Its social base also includes certain sections of the zamindari, the students, the lower middle classes in cities and towns. An attempt was also made to give political expression to the economic grievances of the working class by organizing strikes.
○ **Students** came out in large numbers to propagate and practise swadeshi, and to take a lead in organising picketing of shops selling foreign goods. Student participation was visible in Bengal, Maharashtra, especially in Poona, and in many parts of the South—Guntur, Madras, Salem. ○ **Women**, who were traditionally home-centred, especially those of the urban middle classes, took active part in processions and picketing. From now onwards, they were to play a significant role in the national movement.
○ **Labour Unrest and Trade Unions** In the beginning, some strikes were organised on the issue of rising prices and racial insult, primarily in the foreign owned companies. In Rawalpindi (Punjab), the arsenal and railway workers went on strike led by Lala Lajpat Rai and Ajit Singh. **So,** **statement II is correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II is the correct explanation for Statement**
**I.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Which of the following provisions was/were
introduced by the Morley-Minto Reforms?
I. 	 The members of the Central legislature were
to be directly elected by the popular vote.
II. 	 Separate electorates for Muslims were
provided at the Centre but not at the Provincial level.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Indian Councils Act 1909, or the Morley-Minto reforms, was introduced by the British government in India as a step towards including Indians in government.
● The Morley-Minto Reform introduced the element of election but the elected **members were to be** **elected indirectly**. Local bodies were to elect an electoral college, which in turn would elect members of provincial legislatures, who in turn would elect members of the central legislature. **So, statement I is not** **correct.**
● Morley Minto reforms introduced the concept of **‘separate electorate’.** The introduction of Separate Electorates was a fulfillment of the commitment made by Minto to the Shimla Deputation and a statutory recognition of Muslim separatism.  Under this, the Muslim members were to be elected only by Muslim voters. In the imperial legislative council of 69 members, eight seats were reserved for Muslims. **At the** **provincial level, Muslim candidates were also to be elected by Muslim electorate only. So, statement II** **is not correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
The Barisal Conference of 1906 during the
Swadeshi Movement was organized by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The **Barisal Conference of 1906** was a significant political event held in **Barisal** (now in Bangladesh) during the **Swadeshi Movement**, a time of rising nationalist fervor following the **Partition of Bengal** **(1905)** by Lord Curzon.
● **The Barisal Conference of 1906 was organized by the Bengal unit of the Indian National Congress** under the presidency of Abdul Rasul to oppose the partition of Bengal. The conference was infamous for the brutal lathi charge made by the police on the delegates and the use of the ‘Bande Matram’ slogan for political symbolism. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following organizations never had
their headquarters in India?
I. 	 Ghadar Party
II. 	 Indian Home Rule Society
III. 	Young India Society
IV. 	 Theosophical Society
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Answer: a** **Explanation**:
● **The Ghadr Party** was a revolutionary group organized around a weekly newspaper, The Ghadr, with its **headquarters in San Francisco** and branches along the US coast and in the Far East. **So, point I is** **correct**.
● Shyamji Krishnavarma had started in **London in 1905 an Indian Home Rule Society**— ‘India House’— as a centre for Indian students, a scholarship scheme to bring radical youth from India, and a journal, The Indian Sociologist. Revolutionaries such as Savarkar and Hardayal became members of India House. **So,** **point II is correct.**
● The Abhinav Bharat Mandir, also known as the **Young India Society**, was founded in 1904 by Vinayak Savarkar and Ganesh Damodar Savarkar. Initially established as “Mitra Mela” in **Nasik, Maharashtra**, it was later renamed Abhinav Bharat. **So, point III is not correct.**
● The **Theosophical Society was founded in New York City in 1875** by Helena Blavatsky, a Russian mystic, and Henry Steel Olcott, the society’s first president. The society seeks to bridge the divide between East and West by emphasizing the similarities in human culture**. The society later moved its** **headquarters to India at Adyar, Chennai**. **So, point IV is not correct**. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With regard to Indian support to Britain during
World War I, consider the following statements:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The period of the **First World War (1914–1919)** was a significant phase in the growth and maturing of Indian nationalism. During this time, Indian political responses to India’s involvement in the war were diverse and evolved across different ideological lines.
● **The Moderates supported Britain during World War I as a matter of loyalty and duty,** hoping it would lead to political concessions after the war. **So, statement I is correct.**
● When **Mahatma Gandhi** arrived in India he found that nationalist opinion was opposed to unconditional support for the war effort. Gandhi did not favour a bargain with the government by offering cooperation at a price and said: **"That we have been loyal at a time of stress is no test of fitness for swaraj (self-** **government).** Loyalty is no merit. It is a necessity of citizenship all over the world." **So, statement II is** **correct.**
● **Extremists like Tilak supported the British during World War I** because they hoped that, after the war, the British would reward India with self-government. They did not completely oppose India’s involvement in the war; instead, their support was given with the expectation of political gains for India after the war ended. **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Literature of Ancient India
questions.questionText: Who among the following authored “Stripurushtulna”, a book on gender inequality?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Gender inequality** was a pervasive and deeply entrenched issue in India during the colonial period, affecting almost every aspect of women's lives. While the British introduced some reforms that ostensibly aimed at improving women's status, the overall impact of colonial rule was complex.
● **Tarabai Shinde**, a woman educated at home in Poona, published a book, **Stripurushtulna**, (A Comparison between Women and Men), **criticising the social differences between men and women.** She directly challenged the patriarchal norms, religious scriptures, and social customs that subjugated women, denying them education, autonomy, and dignity. **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**
**Knowledge Box**
● **Devi Chaudhurani (Sarala Ghosal)**was an eminent writer and the **founder of the first** **women’s organization in 1910 (Bharat Stree Mahamandala in Allahabad)**, and a very close associate of Mahatma Gandhi. She worked for women’s education and emancipation. She **edited the Bengali journal "Bharati"** for many years and composed
numerous patriotic **songs**, including **"Utho Go Bharatlakshmi" (Arise, Mother India)**,
which became widely popular.
● **The first female teacher in India** to gain recognition for her contributions to women's education and social rights **was Savitribai Phule.** Additionally, she worked for women from lower social classes. **In 1848, along with Jyotirao Phule, she founded the first** **school for females in India in Pune**.
● **Pandita Ramabai,** a great scholar of Sanskrit, felt that Hinduism was oppressive towards women, and **wrote a book (The High Caste Hindu Woman (1887)** about the miserable lives of upper-caste Hindu women. She founded a widows’ home at Poona to provide shelter to widows who had been treated badly by their husbands’ relatives. Here, women were trained so that they could support themselves economically.

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Chola and other South Indian Kingdoms and Contact with Southeast Asia
questions.questionText:
Consider the following demands:
I. 	 Autonomy in internal and external affairs
II. 	 Expansion of legislative councils
III. 	Education in vernacular languages
IV. 	 Repeal of emergency wartime restrictions
Which of the above were the primary demands of the Home Rule League Movement?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Home Rule Movement was the Indian response to the First World War in a less charged but a more effective way.  **Two Home Rule Leagues were launched—one by Balgangadhar Tilak and the other by Annie Besant,** both with the aim of beginning a new trend of aggressive politics.
● The Home Rule League Movement mainly asked for **self-rule within the British Empire.** This meant **control over internal matters, but not full control over foreign affairs**. Thus, its main aim was not complete Swaraj but Home Rule or internal self–government. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● The movement demanded greater Indian representation and self-government, including **the expansion of** **legislative councils** and the election of their members. **So, statement II is correct.**
● Tilak advocated for **education in vernacular languages to make learning accessible to the broader** **population.** He wanted all the regional languages and cultures to develop, and argued for education to be imparted in the vernaculars. **So, statement III is correct.**
● The movement emerged in reaction to wartime restrictions and repressive measures. People were feeling the burden of wartime miseries caused by high taxation and a rise in prices, and were ready to participate in any aggressive movement of protest. However, **the formal agenda of the Home Rule League did not** **include the repeal of these restrictions as a main demand**. **So, statement IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
He was extremely pained at the poverty and the
misery of his countrymen. He firmly believed that any reform could become successful only by uplifting the condition of the masses. His clarion call to the people of India was to rise above the narrow confines of their ‘religion of the kitchen’ and come together in the service of the nation. By sending out this call, he made a single contribution to the nascent nationalism of India. He was:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Swami Vivekananda (1863–1902)**, born as Narendra Nath Dutta, successfully blended the spiritual simplicity of his guru with a modern and rational worldview, which he propagated not only in India but across the globe. His address at the **World Parliament of Religions in Chicago in 1893** brought him international acclaim, with the New York Herald recognizing the depth of Indian spiritual wisdom by remarking, “We feel how foolish it is to send missionaries to this learned nation.”
● He was the first modern Indian to reassert the global significance of Vedanta philosophy, positioning Indian spirituality on the world stage. His message went beyond religion; he was deeply distressed by the poverty and suffering of the Indian masses. He believed that any meaningful **social reform** must begin with the **upliftment of the common people**, and urged Indians to rise above the **‘religion of the kitchen’,** a metaphor for orthodox, ritualistic practices, and dedicate themselves to **national service**.
● His passionate appeal gave a significant boost to the emerging **nationalist movement in India**, injecting it with a sense of **spiritual pride and collective purpose**. Yet, his vision of nationalism was never parochial.
● He firmly believed in the universal unity of mankind, advocating that the **global problems of humanity** **could be resolved only when nations worked together as equals**. He thus called upon the youth to unite based on India’s shared spiritual legacy, positioning himself as a symbol of new awakening and a guiding force for the future. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
The 1916 Lucknow Session of the Indian National
Congress is remembered for which of the following reasons?
I. 	 It resulted in the official endorsement of
Home Rule Leagues by the Congress.
II. 	 It marked the reunion of the Moderates and
Extremists after nearly a decade of split.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● By early 1915, Annie Besant had launched a campaign to demand self-government for India after the war on the lines of white colonies. Although the concept was discussed during the Indian National Congress’ **Lucknow session of 1916,** **the Congress did not officially approve or adopt the scheme at that time**. Not willing to wait for too long, Besant laid the condition that if the Congress did not implement its commitments, she would be free to set up her own league—which she finally had to, as there was no response from the Congress. **So, statement I is not correct.**
● **The Lucknow Session of 1916** was significant for the reunion of the Moderates and Extremists after nearly a decade of split following the Surat session in 1907. Thus, it was the first session after nine years where both factions came together in an organized manner. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.microTopic: Salient Features of the Indian Constitution & Comparison with Other Countries
questions.questionText:
Consider the following :
I. 	 Self-government should be granted to the
Indian people as early as possible
II. 	 Expansion of the legislative councils with
more elected members
III. 	The right to make appointments in the
Imperial Civil Service should be vested in the Government of India
IV. 	 All of the Viceroy’s and Governors’ executive
council members should be Indians.
How many of the above demands were put forward under the Congress-League scheme of 1916?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The Congress-League Scheme refers to the joint constitutional reform proposals presented by the Indian National Congress and the All India Muslim League in 1916, popularly known as the Lucknow Pact. In this scheme following demands were put forward:
● The government should announce that it will grant **self-government** to Indians soon. **So, point I is** **correct.**
● Central and provincial legislative councils should be **expanded**, with **more elected members** and greater powers. The **term** of legislative councils should be **five years**. **So, point II is correct.**
● It was also demanded in this memorandum that the **right to make appointments in the Imperial** **Administrative Service (Civil Service) should be vested in the Government of India** and appointment of Indian people to the Army and Navy's Commissioned and non- Commissioned posts. **So, point III is** **correct.**
● The salary of the Secretary of State for India should be paid by the British government, not from Indian funds. **Half of the members (not all) in the Viceroy’s and Governors’ executive councils should be** **Indians**. **So, point IV is not correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Early National Movement - Moderates, Extremists
questions.questionText:
The Vandemataram Movement, as part of
the Swadeshi movement, was particularly prominent in:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The partition of Bengal infuriated people all over India. All sections of the Congress – the Moderates and the Radicals – opposed it. Large public meetings and demonstrations were organised and novel methods of mass protest developed. The struggle that unfolded came to be known as the **Swadeshi movement.**
● In regions like **deltaic Andhra**, the anti-partition movement took the form of the **Vandemataram** **Movement**, named after the patriotic song that became its rallying cry. The movement emphasized swadeshi, self-help, national education, and the promotion of Indian languages.
● Radicals advocated mass mobilisation, boycott of British goods and institutions, and some even endorsed revolutionary violence as a path to swaraj. **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Economy
questions.microTopic: Map-Based Relevance (Trade Routes, Ports, Countries, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** The agricultural income in 18th century India was better compared to previous centuries.
**Statement II:** Some of the new states in 18th-century India made efforts to maintain internal trade and promote foreign commerce.
Which one of the following is correct with respect to the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement ll explains Statement I
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct,
but Statement II does **not** explain Statement I
options.c:
Statement I is correct, but Statement II is
incorrect
options.d:
Statement I is incorrect, but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● Constant warfare and disruption of law and order in many areas during the 18th century harmed the commercial activities. Many trading centres were looted by the contestants for power and by foreign invaders. Many of the trade routes were infested with organised bands of robbers, and traders and their caravans were regularly looted.
● The new independent states of 18th century India could not stop the economic decline. The number of **zamindars and jagirdars** kept increasing, and they **fought among themselves over** **shrinking** **agricultural income**. At the same time, the condition of the peasants continued to worsen. This period saw economic decline because of constant warfare, heavy taxation, and unstable administration. **So,** **statement I is not correct.**
● Despite the overall economic crisis, some of the **new states in 18th-century India made efforts to** **maintain internal trade and promote foreign commerce**. However, they took no significant steps to modernize the industrial or commercial infrastructure, and their limited trade initiatives were insufficient to address the deeper economic problems.  **So, statement II is correct.** **So, Statement-I is incorrect, but Statement-II is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
In the context of the Mughal administration,
‘subahs’ were:

options.a:
Small territories given to local chiefs for their
loyalty to the empire
options.b:
Vassal states that remained outside the
Mughal central authority
options.c:
Provinces under the Mughal Empire with
direct administrative control
options.d:
Semi-independent regions not formally part
of Mughal governance

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Subahs** were the main administrative divisions (Mughals administrative provinces)  of the Mughal Empire, created to ensure efficient governance. Each Subah was headed by a **Subedar** (governor), who was supported by a Diwan, responsible for revenue administration. Subahs were further divided into **Sarkars (districts)**, each managed by a **Faujdar**. These Sarkars were split into **Parganas (sub-districts)**,
which consisted of several villages. Key officials at the Pargana level included the **Qanungo** (responsible for maintaining revenue records), the **Chaudhuri** (in charge of revenue collection), and the **Qazi** (judicial officer). The village, being the smallest administrative unit, was overseen by a **Muqaddam**. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following pairs:
Autonomous Kingdom
Founder
I. - Hyderabad Safdar Jung
II. - Bengal Saadat Khan
III. - Awadh Asaf Jah
IV. - Punjab Ranjit Singh
How many of the pairs given above are correctly
matched?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
After the Mughal Empire weakened, many independent and semi-independent states like Bengal, Awadh, Hyderabad, Mysore, and the Maratha Kingdom came up. While some of these states were established by Mughal provincial governors asserting greater autonomy, others originated through active resistance and rebellion against Mughal authority.
● **Hyderabad** was founded in 1724 by **Asaf Jah**, also known as **Nizam-ul-Mulk**, a former Mughal governor. He established an autonomous state while continuing to acknowledge the Mughal emperor nominally.  **So, pair I is not correctly matched.**
● **Bengal** became autonomous under **Murshid Quli Khan**, who was appointed as the Mughal governor of Bengal in the early 18th century. He gradually reduced Mughal control and established independent authority while maintaining nominal allegiance to the emperor. **So, pair II is not correctly matched.**
● **Awadh** was founded by **Saadat Khan** in 1722 when he was appointed as the Mughal governor. Over time, he established a semi-independent rule while still recognizing Mughal sovereignty in name.  **So,** **pair III is not correctly matched.**
● **Ranjit Singh** established the **Sikh Empire** in the early 19th century, with **Lahore** as its capital. He unified various Sikh misls and created a strong, centralized state in the **Punjab region**.  **So, pair IV is correctly** **matched.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.** **Subject:** History | Advent of Europeans in India **Tags:** Factual, Analytical

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With reference to 18th-century India, consider the
following statements:
I. 	 Trivendrum became a famous centre of
Sanskrit scholarship.
II. 	 Jaipur developed as a great seat of science
and art.
Which of the statements given above is/are
correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **In the second half of the 18th century, Trivandrum (the capital of Travancore) emerged as a** **significant centre of Sanskrit scholarship**. This cultural flourishing was largely due to the patronage of the rajas and chiefs of Kerala, who supported literature and learning. Rama Varma, who succeeded Martanda Varma, was an exemplary ruler known for his literary and artistic talents. A poet, scholar, musician, and actor, Rama Varma was deeply cultured and exhibited a cosmopolitan outlook by maintaining an active interest in European affairs. His reign played a vital role in the revival of Malayalam literature and the promotion of Sanskrit scholarship in Trivandrum. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Jaipur developed as a major centre of science and art under the leadership of Raja Sawai Jai Singh** **of Amber, who ruled from 1681 to 1743**. Jai Singh was a remarkable statesman, lawmaker, reformer, and most notably, a pioneering astronomer during a period when scientific pursuits were generally neglected in India. He founded the city of Jaipur, planning it meticulously according to scientific and architectural principles, with wide streets laid out at right angles. Jai Singh established observatories equipped with sophisticated instruments—some of his own invention—at several important locations, including Delhi, Jaipur, Ujjain, Varanasi, and Mathura. His contributions made Jaipur a renowned hub of scientific inquiry and artistic achievement in the 18th century. **So, statement II is correct.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Governor Generals and Their Policies, Administrative Developments, Judicial Developments, Civil Services, Police, Military
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements :
I. 	 The Jagirdari system allocated revenue from
the assigned territory to nobles in lieu of cash salaries for their services to the state.
II. 	 The Saranjami system involved making
hereditary land grants to military commanders in return for services.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● The Jagirdari System was a prominent land revenue assignment system in medieval and early modern India, including during the Mughal period and continued in various forms in the 18th century. **Under this** **system, the state assigned the revenue of a particular area (jagir) to a noble, official, or military** **officer instead of paying them a cash salary for their services.** The jagirdar collected the revenue from the assigned territory and used it to maintain troops, administer the area, and fulfil their obligations to the state. The jagir was generally not hereditary and could be transferred or revoked by the sovereign authority. **So, statement I is correct.**
● The Saranjami system was specific to the Maratha polity. **In this system, land grants (saranjams) were** **given primarily to military commanders and important officials** as compensation for their service,
particularly military service. These grants allowed the recipients to collect revenue from the land and maintain troops for the Maratha state. **Saranjams were initially non-hereditary, but could sometimes** **become hereditary**; they were granted for as long as the holder rendered service. **So, statement II is not** **correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
Which of the following items were imported by
India during the 18th century?
I. 	 Raw silk
II. 	 Spices
III. 	Tea
IV. 	 Ivory
V. 	 Indigo
VI. 	Paper
Select the correct answer using the code given below:

options.d: I, II, III, IV and VI only

questions.explanationMarkdown:
● During the Mughal period, though Indian villages were largely self-sufficient, with minimal imports and limited means of communication, a flourishing trade existed both within the country and between India and various regions of Asia and Europe.
○ **Persian Gulf region:** India imported pearls, **raw silk**, wool, dates, dried fruits, and rose water ○ **Arabia:** coffee, gold, drugs, and honey ○ **China:** **tea**, sugar, porcelain, and silk ○ **Tibet:** musk and woollen cloth ○ **Singapore:** tin ○ **Indonesian islands;** **spices**, perfumes, arrack, and sugar ○ **Africa:** **ivory** and drugs; and ○ **Europe:** woollen cloth, metals such as copper, iron, and lead, and **paper**.
● In the 18th century, **India did not import indigo from outside.** Indigo cultivation has a long history. The plant, Indigofera tinctoria, was cultivated for its dye, which was highly prized for its vibrant blue color. European demand for indigo, particularly in the 18th century, led to its widespread cultivation in places like Bengal, often under exploitative conditions for local farmers. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
In the 18th century, the cities of Surat, Burhanpur,
Varanasi and Masulipatnam were famous as:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**During the 18th century, Surat, Burhanpur, Varanasi, and Masulipatnam were all prominent centres of** **textile production and trade**.
● **Surat,** located on the west coast, was **renowned for its fine cotton textiles and silk fabrics,** which were in high demand both domestically and internationally. It was a major port city facilitating the export of Indian textiles to West Asia, Europe, and Southeast Asia.
● **Burhanpur,** situated in central India, was **famous for producing fine cotton fabrics, particularly** **muslins and calicoes**, which were highly valued in foreign markets.
● **Varanasi (Banaras)** was a significant **centre for silk weaving and the production of luxurious** **brocades and fine textiles**, which continue to be famous even today.
● **Masulipatnam,** located on the Coromandel coast, was **known for its high-quality chintz, calicoes, and** **hand-painted or printed cotton fabrics** that were extensively exported to Southeast Asia and Europe. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: Biology and Biotechnology
questions.questionText: In the context of 18th century India, Marumakkathayam was:

options.a: A martial art practiced in Tamil Nadu
options.b: A dance form popular in Andhra region

questions.explanationMarkdown:
In 18th century India, the dominant family system across most regions was patriarchal, where authority rested with the senior male member of the family, and inheritance passed through the male lineage.
● However, Kerala was a notable exception to this prevailing patriarchal norm**.** **In Kerala, a matrilineal** **system—known locally as Marumakkathayam** among certain communities such as the Nairs—was followed. Under this system, lineage and inheritance were traced through the female line. Property was inherited through the mother’s side, and senior women in the family held significant authority in domestic and property matters. This unique system distinguished Kerala from other regions of India during the 18th century, **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: International Relations
questions.microTopic: India and the World (USA, Russia, Middle East, Africa, etc.)
questions.questionText:
In the history of India, Kunchan Nambiar,
Shah Abdul Latif and Tayaumanavar were well remembered as:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**In 18th century India, Kunchan Nambiar, Shah Abdul Latif, and Tayaumanavar were renowned as poets** **who made significant contributions to the literary traditions of their respective regions**.
● **Kunchan Nambiar,** from Kerala, was **a leading figure in the revival of Malayalam literature.** Under the patronage of the Travancore rulers Martanda Varma and Rama Varma, **he composed poetry in** **simple, colloquial language** that was accessible to the common people. He is also credited with enriching the Ottamthullal performance art, blending humor, social criticism, and poetry.
● **Tayaumanavar**, a Tamil poet from 18th-century Tamil Nadu, was one of the **foremost exponents of** **Sittar poetry.** His works reflected deep spiritual insight while simultaneously criticising the corruption in temple administration and the rigid caste system. **His poetry is noted for its mystical and** **philosophical depth**, which remains influential in Tamil devotional literature.
● **Shah Abdul Latif**, from Sindh, was one of the **greatest poets in the Sindhi language**. His major work, **Risalo**, is a collection of poems that express spiritual devotion, love, and human values. His poetry played a significant role in the cultural and literary development of Sindhi literature during the 18th century. **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements with regard to
Tipu Sultan:
I. 	 He employed rockets with propellants in
wars.
II. 	 He created a copper coin called paisa.
III. 	He laid the foundation for the Kannambadi
dam.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Military Innovation:** The **Mysorean rockets** used by Tipu during the Battle of Pollilur were notably more sophisticated than those previously encountered by the British East India Company. This advancement was largely due to the **incorporation of iron tubes to contain the propellant,** which allowed for greater thrust and extended the missiles' range to nearly 2 kilometers. **So, statement I is** **correct.**
● **Coinage System:** Tipu's coinage is noted for its complexity, introducing new denominations with Persian legends. He continued the issuance of gold pagodas and silver rupees while **creating copper coins called** **paisa. So, statement II is correct.**
● **Economy:** Under Tipu’s leadership, **Mysore experienced a significant economic boom, overtaking** **Bengal Subah as India's dominant economic power.** He focused on enhancing agricultural productivity and textile manufacturing while initiating silk cultivation. **His ambitious projects, like the foundation** **for the Kannambadi dam, aimed to improve the infrastructure and resources of his kingdom**. **So,** **statement III is correct.** **Therefore, option (d) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: Science & Technology
questions.microTopic: IPR, Institutions and Policies for IPR
questions.questionText:
With regard to the administrative reforms of
Lord Cornwallis in India, consider the following statements:
I. 	 He enforced rules against private trade and
acceptance of gifts by officials.
II. 	 He raised the salaries of the civil servants to
reduce corruption.
III. 	He introduced promotions based solely on
merit to make them independent of outside influence.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
Lord **Cornwallis** undertook significant reforms in the Indian Civil Service to tackle widespread corruption and inefficiency among Company officials.
● Recognizing that low salaries and the practice of private trade encouraged dishonesty, he strictly enforced rules **prohibiting private trade** and the acceptance of **bribes and gifts** by Company servants.  **So,** **statement I is correct.**
● At the same time, he **substantially raised their salaries,** making the Company's Civil Service the highest paid in the world. For example, the Collector of a district was paid Rs. 1500 a month along with a one percent commission on revenue collection, which greatly reduced the incentive for corrupt practices. **So,** **statement II is correct.**
● Cornwallis also introduced the principle that **promotion** in the Civil Service would be based on **seniority**, **rather than merit or favoritism**, to ensure that civil servants remained **independent of external** **influence**.  **So, statement III is not correct.** **Therefore, option (a) is the correct answer.**

questions.subject: History
questions.microTopic: Major Dynasties of Early Medieval India
questions.questionText:
Consider the following statements:
**Statement I:** All higher posts in British administration were held by Englishmen only.
**Statement II:** Britain believed that an administration based on British ideas could be firmly established only by English personnel.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a:
Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
and Statement II explains Statement II
options.b:
Both Statement I and Statement II are
correct but Statement II does **not** explain Statement II
options.c:
Statement I is correct but Statement II is not
correct
options.d:
Statement I is not correct but Statement II is
correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Civil Service** in British India was established by **Lord Cornwallis** and initially consisted of East India Company servants who were poorly paid and allowed to engage in private trade.
● A major feature of his reforms was the **rigid and complete exclusion of Indians** **from higher** **administrative posts**. It was officially laid down in 1793 that all senior positions in administration worth more than a specified amount were reserved exclusively for the English. **So, statement I is correct.**
● **Why did the British follow such a policy? Many factors combined to produce it.** o For one, **they were convinced that an administration based on British ideas, institutions, and** **practices could be firmly established only by English personnel.** And, then, they did not trust the ability and integrity of the Indians. Although they did not trust the ability and integrity of Indians, Indians were  equally capable of running large administrations. This was visible from the size of the Mughal Empire and Vijayanagar Kingdom, solely run by native Indians. **So, statement II is correct.** **So, both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement II.** **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Which of the following modern legal concepts
were introduced by the British in India?
I. 	 Rule of Law
II. 	 Equality before Law
III. 	Common Law System
IV. 	 Independent judiciary
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **British introduced several major reforms in India** that fundamentally altered its administrative, legal, and social structure. They reorganised the administrative system by gradually removing Indians from key posts and introducing a system based on the English pattern.
● **The Rule of Law:** The British introduced the modern concept of the rule of law. This meant that **their** **administration was to be carried out, at least in theory, in obedience to laws,** which clearly defined the rights, privileges, and obligations of the subjects and not according to the caprice or personal discretion of the ruler.  **So, point I is correct.**
● **Equality before Law:** The Indian legal system under the British was based on the concept of equality before law. This meant that **in the eyes of law all men were equal.** The **same law applied to all persons** **irrespective of their caste, religion, or clas**s. Previously, the judicial system had paid heed to caste
distinctions and had differentiated between the so-called high-born and low-born. For the same crime lighter punishment was awarded to a Brahmin than to a non-Brahmin. **So, point II is correct.**
● **Common Law System:** A common law system, also known as a case law system, **relies on judicial** **precedents rather than codified legislation**. It's a legal framework where court decisions establish principles and rules that guide future cases. The British laid the foundations of a new system of dispensing justice through a hierarchy of civil and criminal courts. Though given a start by Warren Hastings, the system was stabilised by Cornwallis in 1793.  **So, point III is correct.**
● During British rule in India, **the judiciary lacked true independence** and was frequently subordinate to the executive, especially in the early phases of colonial administration. This was evident through the executive's control over the appointment and removal of judges, the absence of secure tenure, and the widespread reliance on "acting" judges who served at the discretion of the government. **So, point IV is** **not correct.** **Therefore, option (b) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Who among the following was the founder of the
Tatvabodhini Sabha?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
The **Tattvabodhini Sabha** was established as a group dedicated to the rational and systematic study of India's past, particularly in the **Bengali language**. It served as the organ of the Sabha, promoting these ideas and fostering rational thought among the Bengali intelligentsia.
● **Debendranath Tagore** combined the finest elements of traditional Indian learning with Western ideas. In **1839**, he founded the **Tattvabodhini Sabha** to promote the reformist ideas of **Ram Mohan Roy**. Figures like Akshay Kumar Dutt also contributed to its journalistic and reformist efforts.
● The Tattvabodhini Sabha helped: ○ Reorganize and revitalize the Brahmo Samaj under Debendranath Tagore in 1843. ○ Support progressive causes like widow remarriage, abolition of polygamy, women's education, and improvement of the condition of the ryots (peasants). ○ The Sabha’s activism was instrumental in spreading the Brahmo movement across Bengal. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**

questions.questionText:
Which one of the following had Euclid's "Elements
of Geometry", translated into Sanskrit as also several works on trigonometry, and Napier's work on the construction and use of logarithms?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
● **Jai Singh** was above everything a **great astronomer. He erected observatories with accurate and** **advanced instruments, some of them of his own invention, at Delhi, Jaipur, Ujjain, Varanasi, and** **Mathura.** His astronomical observations were remarkably accurate. He drew up a set of tables, entitled Zij Muhammadshahi, to enable people to make astronomical observations.
● He had **Euclid's "Elements of Geometry", translated into Sanskrit** as also several works on trigonometry, and **Napier's work on the construction and use of logarithms**. **Therefore, option (c) is the correct answer.**
